summary: Virginia M.-Y. Lee recounts her non-traditional path to becoming a leading neuroscientist. She emphasizes the importance of pursuing one's true interests despite societal or familial expectations, the necessity of mentorship for underrepresented groups, and how resilience and hard work paved the way for her success in academia.

principles.statement: Pursue your own interests over societal and gender expectations.
principles.rationale: Traditional expectations can stifle your potential and push you into paths you dislike. You must actively ask yourself what you want and pursue it.
principles.source_quote: But as a woman you really need to ask yourself what do you wanna do? What do you like to do? And you've gotta pursue that.

principles.statement: Mentorship is vital for those without natural role models.
principles.rationale: Navigating a scientific career is difficult, and having someone to guide you is crucial when you don't see people like yourself in the field.
principles.source_quote: Now I mentor graduate students and post ops and you know fellows. It's so important because I never really had much of a role model.

principles.statement: Science offers an equal playing field.
principles.rationale: Even if you are at a disadvantage in other areas, such as language proficiency, science and math provide a universal opportunity to excel.
principles.source_quote: I looked around the class and I said well they're better than me in English. But I said to myself well we all have the equal opportunity for science.

mental_models.name: The Workload Benchmark
mental_models.description: Using a period of extreme, successful effort as a baseline to calibrate self-confidence for future challenges.
mental_models.example: When she successfully juggled securing two grants while simultaneously completing an Executive MBA at Wharton, she used that achievement as proof that she had the capacity to succeed in academic science.

heuristics: Either you shut up or you get out (when complaining about a bad job situation, take action rather than just venting).

signature_quotes.text: But as a woman you really need to ask yourself what do you wanna do? What do you like to do? And you've gotta pursue that.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on overcoming her mother's traditional expectations for her as an Asian woman.

signature_quotes.text: I said to myself well we all have the equal opportunity for science.
signature_quotes.context: Realizing that despite being behind her peers in English, she could compete and excel in the sciences.

signature_quotes.text: So I said to myself if I can do that I should be able to be successful in science for crying out loud.
signature_quotes.context: After successfully getting two grants while enrolled in an Executive MBA program at Wharton.

signature_quotes.text: Working in science is difficult to do but my scientific career worked out really well. It's really keeping me from aging quickly.
signature_quotes.context: Concluding thoughts on her long and successful career in neuroscience.

anti_patterns.description: Working for leaders who do not understand the science.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It leads to frustration and a lack of direction, as she experienced during her brief, unhappy stint in industry where her boss didn't know what was going on.

anti_patterns.description: Conforming to traditional gender expectations to avoid being seen as 'too dominant'.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It forces you into careers you have no talent or passion for, such as when she was sent to study piano just to keep her out of the sciences.
